data.title: The WSJ Dollar Index Rises 0.61% This Week to 95.56 — Data Talk
data.description:
The WSJ Dollar Index is up 0.58 point or 0.61% this week to 95.56 




     --Up for two consecutive weeks 



     --Up 1.35 points or 1.44% over the last two weeks 



     --Largest two-week point and percentage gain since the week ending Dec 20, 2024 



     --Today it is down 0.12 point or 0.13% 



     --Down two of the past three trading days 



     --Off 9.12% from its record close of 105.14 hit Tuesday, Sept. 27, 2022 



     --Off 7.77% from its 52-week high of 103.61 hit Friday, Jan. 10, 2025

data.title: I have early Alzheimer's and my husband has stage 4 kidney disease. We just inherited $50K. How can this help us?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'We have a 401(k) with $20,000, and $29,000 in a Roth IRA'

Dear Quentin,

My husband recently received a $50,000 inheritance. We have no idea what to do with it. We want to be wise, but everything is so confusing. I have early Alzheimer's and have trouble organizing my thoughts. My husband is autistic and something of a savant, brilliant with math and computers but verbally challenged.

The company he works for lost its federal funding and may close after March 2026. We're hopeful new funding will be found but want to assume, for the sake of planning, that it closes. I have artwork and rare books, which I will start selling over eBay, and conservatively expect to make $7,000. That would be our moving money (fingers crossed).

We have a 401(k) with $20,000, and $29,000 in a Roth IRA. Our credit-card debt at the moment is close to zero.

So it's up to me to explain:

1. My husband is 70 and just started receiving Social Security - $4,900 a month. My Social Security is $335 after paying for Medicare parts A and B. He is in the process of signing up for Medicare. Right now we have private insurance, Aetna, which is great, through his company. He brings home $7,200 a month.

2. We would like to move near our kids in Norfolk, Va., if possible at some point. But if not, that's OK. We live in Oklahoma now. If we move to Norfolk, we'd buy a decent mobile home and live on our son's property. There are great homes we love in the $40,000 range.

3. Our house used to be paid off, but we ran up huge bills helping one of our sons fight for custody of his children. It cost us $60,000 in lawyer fees and other costs. We put the debt on credit cards and mortgaged the house to pay them off. We do not regret a penny of it. We were desperate. We have a mortgage for $56,000 at 7.7%. Our house is barely worth $63,000.

4. We pay $500 a month for our car, but it will be paid off early next year.

5. My husband has term-life insurance, worth about $400,000, which will end at some point, but I don't know when. He is insured through his job for $100,000. I'm insured for $25,000. That ends when the job does.

'Our home insurance charges us about $300 a month over what we should pay. But I'm afraid to get a new policy.'

6. Our home insurance charges us about $300 a month over what we should pay. But I'm afraid to get a new policy because the new company's agent would assess the house and it needs work to actually be insurable. Then our company might somehow find out how bad the roof and other stuff is, and drop us.

7. My husband was just diagnosed with stage 4 kidney disease. We're hoping to reverse it. He has long-term disability through his company as long as he's working full time. He takes seven medications daily and I take 15. Our co-pays are really good through Aetna. I don't know what the cost would be on Medicare.

8. When my Alzheimer's starts to get bad, we'll establish residency in a state that allows death with dignity and I will end my life peacefully, probably in Washington, D.C., where we have family that would help with the residency part. I feel lucky to have that beautiful end-of-life option; no complaints here. And the disease is progressing astonishingly slowly, I am told.

9. We don't have any of the paperwork that we should, like a "do not resuscitate" order and medical powers of attorney and all that stuff. It looks so complicated.

10. I'm looking for ways to make money from home that are uncomplicated. I think I could do online customer care, but I'd need a computer. But what if I buy a computer, but can't seem to wrap my head around the job? Then the computer purchase would be wasted money. I babysit a dog daily for $200 a month, but I can't handle another dog.

What do you think of our plan?

Wife & Mother

Don't miss: My daughter has $500K in med-school expenses. Can my wife and I afford to pay it off?

Dear Wife & Mother,

You lived a good life, you're a team, you fought for your son when he needed your help, and you are making plans now for the next chapter of your lives. Your children will welcome you home to Norfolk, and you can take heart that you all did the best you could - and it was enough. People often talk about the American dream in terms of homeownership and wealth and success, but you represent the American dream too.

That $50,000 inheritance has arrived on time. It will give you a cushion should you need it after you sell your house and move to Norfolk. You have a lot in your favor: Zero debt is a huge plus. And it's a smart move to sell belongings before your move. It will help subsidize your transport costs and also help reduce them. Your husband has a very high Social Security benefit and will hopefully be able to continue with his job until you leave.

People talk about the American dream in terms of homeownership and wealth, but you represent it too.

You both sound like good, practical people. You've both been saddled with unwelcome diagnoses, but I salute you for the way you have handled this news and have continued to forge ahead and plan your lives. You don't have to set up residency in all states to avail of "death with dignity" - also known as "medical aid in dying" - laws. Vermont and Oregon no longer have a residency requirement.

Making sure you have the necessary documents in place before you enter mid- or late-stage Alzheimer's will prevent complications later on, like your husband or one of your children applying to be your guardian or conservator, which can be a lengthy and complicated process. The National Institute on Aging outlines several signs that a person is no longer able to look after their own finances.

A 10-point review:

1. You should be able to get up to 50% of your spouse's Social Security under the spousal-benefits rules. Given that you have early-onset Alzheimer's, you likely qualify for Medicare, assuming your spouse leaves his job and exits his employer-based health insurance, even if you are not yet 65, according to the Alzheimer's Association.

2. Between your $50,000 windfall and the sale of your house, I hope you find a comfortable mobile home for use on your son's property. He will be lucky to have you. It's probably not worth spending money on your house before you sell it, given the modest price you will get for it, and it will be another big win to rid yourself of that 7.7% mortgage rate.

3. It seems like your son needed help to extricate himself and his family from a toxic situation. You did what you felt was right at the time and, if you helped give your grandchildren a safer and happier environment, it was worth it for all of you. The best part: Now you will be able to enjoy your family with the time you both have left.

4. More good timing with paying off your car loan. Brava!

5. After term-life insurance expires, it converts to renewable term-life insurance, which may go up every year, based on your age and other risk factors.

You have a lot in your favor: Zero debt is a huge plus. And it's a smart move to sell belongings before your move.

6. I agree with you on your home insurance. Stick with what you have until you sell.

7. Medicare does cover the majority of dialysis costs for people with end-stage renal disease, but out-of-pocket expenses like deductibles, coinsurance and copayments add up. (Read more here.) A Medicare Advantage plan or Medicare Part C offers services from private companies approved by Medicare. Contact your State Health Insurance Assistance Program for more help.

8. You're not alone. Nearly 7 million Americans have Alzheimer's disease, which is one of many forms of dementia. Nearly two-thirds of people with Alzheimer's are women. As the U.S. population ages, so too will the number of Americans with dementia, the Alzheimer's Association says. By 2050, the number is projected to reach 12.7 million.

9. I urge you to get your documents in order while you are well enough to do so, including the "do not resuscitate" measures, living will, durable power of attorney and healthcare directive. These will all help ensure you have a smooth transition. You can also add your son as a second power of attorney. Your son may be able to help you fill out the relevant forms.

10. Keep it simple. Starting a side business with a new computer now sounds like an additional task you don't need. If you feel unable to take on a second dog, adding a new side hustle with unfamiliar technology sounds far too challenging. I applaud your entrepreneurial spirit; however, completing your No. 9 tasks one by one is a priority.

What's left to say? You are embarking on a journey that takes equal parts courage and equal parts surrender. Keep enjoying the small things in life because, as I'm sure you know, that's where the joy and reward lies. You have a lot of personal administration ahead of you, but we all eat one slice of cake at a time. Good luck and godspeed.

Related: I'm administrator of my sister's estate. Her bank won't tell me the names of her beneficiaries. Is that legal?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com, and follow Quentin Fottrell on X, the platform formerly known as Twitter.

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

I met a friend for lunch. When the check arrived, she said, 'Thank you so much for paying!' Was I taken for a fool?

'She has been telling him lies': My sister convinced my father to sign everything over to her. What can I do?

My father died, leaving everything to my 90-year-old stepmother. Do I have a right to ask her if I'm in her will?

data.title: My husband's brother left his $600K estate to a friend. Can we contest the will? He experienced oxygen deprivation due to COPD.
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'Around 30 days before passing, he changed everything to go to a friend'

Dear Quentin,

My husband is considering contesting his brother's will. Around 30 days before passing, his brother changed everything to go to a friend. He was in and out of the hospital the last few months of his life with COPD. He definitely had some issues with mental capacity due to oxygen deprivation. My husband is still weighing his options and is unsure of how to proceed. His brother had an estate worth $600,000.

Can he suggest mediation or does he have to actually contest the will first? Thank you in advance (TYIA).

Wife & Sister-in-Law

Don't miss: 'We are acquaintances, not friends': My neighbors need somewhere to stay after a house fire. Do I say yes?

Dear Sister-in-Law,

You're very welcome in advance (YVWIA).

Yes and yes. Assuming his brother had no wife or kids, your husband can pursue mediation before he contests the will. And yes, he can contest it on the basis that your brother-in-law allegedly lacked testamentary capacity due to oxygen deprivation. It will be an expensive and laborious process and will likely require testimony from his doctors and lawyers and possibly access to his medical records. It won't be pretty, but the payoff could be significant, even after legal fees.

Whether it's worth it is up to your husband, of course. And whether he succeeds will be up to the judge - and the gods. He will have an uphill battle ahead of him, because the burden of proof rests not on your brother-in-law's friend's shoulders, but on your husband's. Good luck with that! Remember, a person can have diminished capacity and still understand the contents of an important legal document like a will or a trust.

I have some questions: Does your husband truly believe that his brother did not know what he was doing? Does he have evidence that his brother made the new will as a result of undue influence? Does he have reason to believe that the will was improperly executed? (That could be an easier route, if it was indeed the case.) Or does he merely believe that he has an opportunity for a slice of this $600,000 and, rightly or wrongly, he feels aggrieved that his brother cut him out of his will at the 11th hour?

I have received letters about elderly relatives changing their will or writing a new one that raise concerns. One reader wrote to say her millionaire cousin's nursing home coerced her into changing her will - and selling her house. That looked to be a clear breach of ethics and fiduciary duty. In other cases, children have cried foul when a second spouse inherits a parent's entire estate. These tales raise another question: Was your husband's relationship with his brother a healthy and happy one?

Reasons for contesting a will

Lack of testamentary capacity is a commonly cited reason for contesting a will, according to Kimura London & White, a law firm with offices in California and New York, but the contestor would need to produce compelling evidence. "It hinges on whether the deceased person [known as the testator] was of sound mind when they signed their will. Were they fully aware of the assets they possessed and the individuals who would be impacted by their decisions?" the law firm asks.

"If, for instance, medical records or eyewitness accounts reveal that the testator suffered from dementia, Alzheimer's, or another condition affecting their cognitive abilities at the time of signing, the will's validity could be questioned," the law firm adds. You will need to gather solid evidence to help with a lack-of-capacity claim. "But advanced age, feebleness, illness, or even eccentricity does not mean he lacked the capacity."

Proving undue influence could be difficult. Was your brother-in-law isolated by this friend? Was it a new person on the scene - a neighbor, tenant, lover or even a caregiver? Did they keep your brother-in-law from contact with family members and other friends, or in some way exploit him emotionally or financially? Again, the burden of proof lies with your brother, so he should be very sure about spending money on a lawyer before embarking on legal action.

"Undue influence may involve convincing the deceased to change beneficiaries or alter the distribution of assets to benefit the person applying pressure," Kimura London & White adds. "Imagine a scenario where a new spouse isolates an elderly person from family and friends, controlling their finances and access to information. If there's a sudden shift in the will's provisions in favor of that spouse, undue influence could be suspected."

Suspicion alone won't overturn the terms of a will.

Related: When my friend was on her deathbed, her parents took $25,000 in cash and won't give it to her son. Do we tell him?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com, and follow Quentin Fottrell on X, the platform formerly known as Twitter.

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Previous columns by Quentin Fottrell:

I'm a stay-at-home mom. Do I take a part-time job to spend more time with my kids - or get a job for six figures?

data.title: DIH Appoints Dennis Streppa to DIH Holding US, Inc. Board of Directors
data.description:
NORWELL, Mass., July 18, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- DIH Holding US, Inc. (“DIH”)(NASDAQ:DHAI), a global provider of advanced robotic devices used in rehabilitation, which incorporate visual stimulation in an interactive manner to enable clinical research and intensive functional rehabilitation and training in patients with walking impairments, reduced balance and/or impaired arm and hand functions, today announced the appointment of Dennis Streppa to its Board of Directors and filling the Audit Committee Chair position.

Dennis Streppa is a seasoned global Chief Operations Officer and entrepreneur with expertise spanning public and private healthcare companies. He’s driven M&A value at Cardinal Health, led manufacturing P&L for an $800M business, tripled margins as an entrepreneur, and spearheaded multi-million-dollar private equity deals. He holds an MBA from Kellogg and specializes in business transformation, cross-functional leadership, and optimizing operational results.

CEO, Jason Chen, stated: “We are thrilled to welcome Dennis Streppa to our Board of Directors as Chair of the Audit Committee. Dennis brings a rare combination of operational rigor, strategic vision, and deep experience in both public and private healthcare companies. His track record in driving growth, overseeing complex integrations, and building accountable teams will be invaluable as we continue to scale and strengthen our governance.”

Mr. Streppa commented on the appointment: “I am truly honored to join the Board of Directors and serve as Chair of the Audit Committee. I look forward to working with this talented team to strengthen governance, drive accountability, and help create value for our stakeholders. I’m excited to contribute my operational and strategic experience in supporting DIH’s success.”

The Board of Directors now comprises five members, with three independent members, further strengthening the company’s commitment to strong governance.

About DIH Holding US, Inc.

DIH stands for the vision to “Deliver Inspiration & Health” to improve the daily lives of millions of people with disabilities and functional impairments through providing devices and solutions enabling intensive rehabilitation. DIH is a global provider of advanced robotic devices used in physical rehabilitation, which incorporate visual stimulation in an interactive manner to enable clinical research and intensive functional rehabilitation and training in patients with walking impairments, reduced balance and/or impaired arm and hand functions. Built through the mergers of global-leading niche technology providers, DIH is a transformative rehabilitation solutions provider and consolidator of a largely fragmented and manual-labor-driven industry.

Caution Regarding Forward-Looking Statements

This press release contains certain statements which are not historical facts, which are forward-looking statements within the meaning of the federal securities laws, for the purposes of the safe harbor provisions under The Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. These forward-looking statements include certain statements made with respect to the business combination, the services offered by DIH and the markets in which it operates, and DIH’s projected future results. These forward-looking statements generally are identified by the words “believe,” “project,” “expect,” “anticipate,” “estimate,” “intend,” “strategy,” “future,” “opportunity,” “plan,” “may,” “should,” “will,” “would,” “will be,” “will continue,” “will likely result,” and similar expressions. Forward-looking statements are predictions provided for illustrative purposes only, and projections and other statements about future events that are based on current expectations and assumptions and, as a result, are subject to risks and uncertainties that could cause the actual results to differ materially from the expected results. These risks and uncertainties include, but are not limited to: general economic, political and business conditions; the ability of DIH to achieve its projected revenue, the failure of DIH realize the anticipated benefits of the recently-completed business combination and access to sources of additional debt or equity capital if needed. While DIH may elect to update these forward-looking statements at some point in the future, DIH specifically disclaims any obligation to do so.

Investor Contact
Louisa Smith
Investor.relations@dih.com

data.title: Trump signs the Genius Act into law, in significant step for U.S. crypto regulation
data.description:
By Gordon Gottsegen

The measure will bring new regulations to stablecoins

As the federal government's self-proclaimed "crypto week" comes to an end, the U.S. has emerged with two crypto bills moving to the Senate and one new piece of crypto legislation signed into law.

On Friday afternoon, President Donald Trump signed the Genius Act, which aims to regulate the issuing and use of stablecoins. Stablecoins are digital assets that are tied to real-world assets - like USDC (USDCUSD) and Tether (USDTUSD), which are pegged to the U.S. dollar DXY - with an aim of keeping their value relatively fixed. This contrasts with some other cryptocurrencies, like bitcoin (BTCUSD), which have historically been more volatile.

Read: Genius Act ushers in a new era for stablecoins. Here's what that means for Americans' wallets.

Trump invited a handful of crypto-industry executives to the signing, including Coinbase Global Inc. (COIN) Chief Executive Brian Armstrong, Kraken CEO Dave Ripley and Robinhood Markets Inc. (HOOD) CEO Vlad Tenev. Also in attendence were Tyler and Cameron Winklevoss, who co-lead crypto exchange Gemini, which in June filed draft paperwork to go public.

"Let me say, the entire crypto community for years, you were mocked and dismissed and counted out," Trump told the audience in the White House's East Room. "But this signing is a massive validation of your hard work and your pioneering spirit and your ability to never give up."

Jennifer Schulp, the director of financial-regulation studies at the Cato Institute, said that Trump's signing of the Genius Act creates an important regulatory framework for stablecoins.

"The good? Stablecoin users will benefit from regulatory clarity that will help to ensure that the coin is stable as promised. The bad? The legislation places unnecessary limitations on competition, grants regulators too much discretion, and leaves open the possibility of increased government surveillance of stablecoin users," Schulp said.

Rajeev Bamra, associate managing director for digital economy at Moody's Ratings, also focused on how the new law will impact the adoption of stablecoins by the crypto industry.

"The act sets a necessary compliance baseline, but whether stablecoins can deliver their promise will depend on how effectively stablecoin issuers disclose reserves, implement operational safeguards, maintain robust governance practices, and ensure structural alignment between issuer interests and consumer protection," Bamra said.

Crucially for Trump, the hope is that stablecoins will become a major buyer of short-term Treasury debt. Trump's signature budget law that passed in July boosted the U.S. debt ceiling by $5 trillion and is estimated to add another $3.4 trillion to the U.S. deficit over the next decade.

"This will increase demand for U.S. Treasurys, lower interest rates and secure the dollar's status as the world's reserve currency for generations to come. The reserve currency is so important. You know, if we lost that, that would be like losing a world war," Trump said.

Read: Now that the megabill has passed, expect a ton of short-term debt to be sold to finance the government's deficit

But as Schulp pointed out, perhaps the most important takeaway for crypto investors is that the government is taking steps to support financial innovation and making the U.S. a global competitor in the crypto world.

Bitcoin was trading lower after Trump signed the Genius Act, down about 1% on the day to $117,891. As Trump has championed the Genius Act since its conception, crypto investors may have assumed the bill's signing was a given once it moved through the House. Bitcoin hit an all-time high of $123,165.67 on Monday and has hovered near those levels all week.

Although bitcoin is the largest digital asset by market capitalization, it's not the only crypto that moved in response to the new legislation. Ether (ETHUSD) was up 19.1% over the past seven days, according to CryptoQuant. Meanwhile, solana (SOLUSD) was up 8% and Ripple's XRP (XRPUSD) was up 21.7% over that same time period.

The upswing in prices this week helped push the value of the entire crypto market above $4 trillion for the first time, as Bloomberg reported. While bitcoin accounts for 60% of this value, other crypto tokens have also played a role in the crypto market's gains.

-Gordon Gottsegen

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  07-18-25 1701ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Broadmark Realty Capital Inc. Investors: Please contact the Portnoy Law Firm to recover your losses; July 28, 2025 Deadline to file Lead Plaintiff Motion
data.description:
Investors can contact the law firm at no cost to learn more about recovering their losses

LOS ANGELES, July 18, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- The Portnoy Law Firm advises Broadmark Realty Capital Inc. ("Broadmark" or the "Company") (NYSE: BRMK) common stock as of the record date of the May 2023 merger between Broadmark and Ready Capital Corporation. Broadmark investors have until July 28, 2025 to file a lead plaintiff motion.

Investors are encouraged to contact attorney Lesley F. Portnoy, by phone 310-692-8883 or email: lesley@portnoylaw.com, to discuss their legal rights, or click here to join the case. The Portnoy Law Firm can provide a complimentary case evaluation and discuss investors’ options for pursuing claims to recover their losses.

CASE ALLEGATIONS: Broadmark Realty Capital Inc. and Ready Capital Corporation are real estate investment trusts (REITs). On May 30, 2023, Broadmark shareholders voted to approve a merger between the two companies, which was finalized on May 31, 2023.

The Broadmark class action lawsuit alleges that the proxy statement used to solicit shareholder approval for the merger contained materially false and/or misleading statements and omitted key facts, including that:

  1. A significant portion of borrowers in Ready Capital’s loan portfolio were facing financial distress due to rising interest rates, which substantially increased their borrowing costs;

  2. An oversupply of multifamily properties in Ready Capital’s core markets limited borrowers’ ability to raise rents sufficiently to offset their growing debt obligations;

  3. A major development project—specifically, a Ritz-Carlton in Portland, Oregon—acquired through Ready Capital’s purchase of Mosaic Real Estate Credit entities (Mosaic Real Estate Credit, LLC; Mosaic Real Estate Credit TE, LLC; and MREC International Incentive Split, LP) had been plagued by severe setbacks, including cost overruns, construction delays, and funding shortfalls. This project represented approximately $500 million of Ready Capital’s acquired loan portfolio;

  4. As a result of these issues, Ready Capital’s Current Expected Credit Loss (CECL) reserves and projected credit losses were significantly understated; and

  5. Accordingly, Ready Capital’s financial projections—including estimates of Distributable Earnings per share, dividends per share, and book value per share—lacked a reasonable basis when issued.

The complaint notes that the price of Ready Capital stock remained significantly below the merger valuation at the time the lawsuit was filed.

The plaintiff is represented by Robbins Geller Rudman & Dowd LLP, a firm with extensive experience in prosecuting investor class actions involving financial fraud.

data.title: Organon & Co. Investors: Please contact the Portnoy Law Firm to recover your losses; July 22, 2025 Deadline to file Lead Plaintiff Motion
data.description:
Investors can contact the law firm at no cost to learn more about recovering their losses

LOS ANGELES, July 18, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- The Portnoy Law Firm advises Organon & Co. ("Organon" or the "Company") (NYSE: OGN) investors of a class action representing investors that bought securities between November 3, 2022 and April 30, 2025, inclusive (the "Class Period"). Organon investors have until July 22, 2025 to file a lead plaintiff motion.

Investors are encouraged to contact attorney Lesley F. Portnoy, by phone 310-692-8883 or email: lesley@portnoylaw.com, to discuss their legal rights, or click here to join the case. The Portnoy Law Firm can provide a complimentary case evaluation and discuss investors’ options for pursuing claims to recover their losses.

CASE ALLEGATIONS: Organon is a healthcare company that develops and delivers prescription therapies and medical devices.

According to the class action lawsuit, throughout the Class Period, defendants made false and/or misleading statements and/or failed to disclose material facts, including that:
(i) Organon concealed critical information regarding its capital allocation strategy—particularly its plans for the quarterly dividend payout;
(ii) despite public statements positioning the dividend as the company’s “number one priority,” Organon had adopted a debt reduction strategy that conflicted with maintaining the dividend; and
(iii) following its acquisition of Dermavant Sciences Ltd., Organon intended to shift capital priorities toward debt reduction, leading to a substantial dividend cut.

The Complaint further alleges that on May 1, 2025, Organon released its Q1 2025 financial results and announced a sharp reduction in its quarterly dividend—from $0.28 to $0.02 per share. On this news, Organon’s stock declined more than 27%, according to the Complaint.

Please visit our website to review more information and submit your transaction information.
